title: Agentic RL for LLMs: Survey Summary

cards.title: Planning before RL (Prompted/Modular)
cards.summary: Planning is scaffolded by prompting and modular decomposition, with little adaptation from experience.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, planning means splitting problem solving into two stages: first generate an explicit plan, then execute it step-by-step, instead of directly generating a final answer in one pass.
cards.how_it_works: Run multiple invocations of the same LLM with different role prompts (planner, checker, coordinator) to draft, verify, and refine steps before execution. You can inspect outcomes at each step and iterate, but improvement is mostly manual prompt/rule editing rather than learned policy updates.

example.flow: Template: Decompose -> Verify prerequisites -> Execute step -> Check result -> Continue

why_works: Easy to implement and inspect
Works well on known plan templates
why_fails: No principled learning from long-horizon outcomes

cards.title: RL as External Guide for Planning
cards.summary: LLM proposes actions while RL-trained value/heuristic functions guide search.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, planning means adding a learned guidance layer on top of generation: the model proposes candidate steps, and a learned value signal decides which partial plan is worth continuing.
cards.how_it_works: Use the LLM for branch proposal, not final one-shot answering. Collect trajectories, train a value function on outcomes, then run search (e.g., MCTS) where branch expansion/pruning is driven by that value model before executing the chosen plan.

example.flow: LLM proposes branches -> value model scores branch states -> search keeps best branches -> execute best plan
example.code:
for _ in range(N):
    tree = init_search_tree(state)
    for _ in range(B):
        node = select_node_ucb(tree)
        actions = llm_propose_actions(node.state)        # explicit action candidates
        children = expand_node(node, actions)
        score_children_with_value(children, value_model) # V(s') guidance
        backup_scores_to_root(children)
    best_traj = extract_best_plan(tree)
    final_reward = execute_plan(best_traj)
    buffer.append((best_traj, final_reward))

train_value_model_from_trajectories(value_model, buffer)

why_works: Improves search efficiency under fixed compute
Keeps base LLM stable while adding adaptive guidance
why_fails: Search-time overhead can still be large

cards.title: RL as Internal Driver of Planning
cards.summary: LLM is directly treated as policy and optimized from interaction outcomes.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, planning means training the generator itself to become a better planner, instead of only using an external search/value module to steer it.
cards.how_it_works: Treat each plan step (including optional tool actions) as policy actions from the LLM. Collect trajectories with rewards or pairwise preferences, then update model weights with PPO/DPO-style objectives so future planning behavior improves directly.

example.flow: Rollout plan trajectories -> attach reward/preference feedback -> optimize policy weights -> repeat
example.code:
for _ in range(N):
    traj = rollout_with_policy(model, env)
    buffer.append(traj)
batch = make_training_batch(buffer)
update_policy(model, batch)  # PPO/DPO-family step

why_works: Internalizes planning behavior across tasks
Can jointly optimize quality and system cost
why_fails: Training stability in open environments remains hard

cards.title: Planning Frontier: Deliberation + Intuition Synthesis
cards.summary: Unify fast intuitive planning with slow deliberate search via a learned meta-policy.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, planning means learning both what to do and how much deliberation to allocate, so the model can switch between fast intuition and slow structured search.
cards.how_it_works: Introduce a meta-controller over planning modes (fast act, branch, prune, tool-check, commit). At each step, the controller chooses the mode, and training rewards task success while charging for compute/latency to avoid unnecessary overthinking.

example.flow: Observe state/uncertainty -> choose planning mode -> execute mode -> repeat until commit
example.code:
MODES = ['fast_act', 'explore_branch', 'prune', 'tool_check', 'commit']
for t in range(T):
    mode = meta_policy.sample(state, history, uncertainty)
    state = apply_mode(mode, state)
    if mode == 'commit':
        break
reward = task_success(state) - lam * compute_used
update_meta_policy(reward)

why_works: Balances speed and robustness per task
Treats planning depth as a learnable control variable
why_fails: Can overthink without good compute penalties

cards.title: ReAct-style Tool Calling
cards.summary: Tool use is induced by prompting or SFT over static interaction traces.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, tool use means turning free-form generation into an explicit think-act-observe loop where the model calls tools between reasoning steps.
cards.how_it_works: Use prompt templates or SFT traces to teach a fixed interaction pattern: reason, emit a tool call, read tool output, continue reasoning, then answer. Behavior is mostly imitation of known patterns, not outcome-optimized policy learning.
why_works: Reliable syntax and invocation patterns
Simple pipeline with no RL infra
why_fails: Imitates static patterns instead of optimizing outcomes

cards.title: Tool-integrated RL (TIR)
cards.summary: Tool calls become policy actions optimized for end-task reward.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, tool use becomes RL action selection: each token/tool decision is part of one policy optimized for task outcomes.
cards.how_it_works: Run trajectories where the model can emit either normal text or tool calls. Environment/tool responses produce transitions and rewards; train with PPO/GRPO-style updates so the policy learns when tools are worth calling and how to chain them effectively.

example.code:
for episode in range(N):
    traj = []
    state = env.reset()
    while not state.done:
        action = policy.sample(state)  # token or tool_call
        next_state, reward = env.step(action)
        traj.append((state, action, reward, next_state))
        state = next_state
    update_policy_with_advantage(policy, traj)

why_works: Learns when/how to call tools adaptively
Deterministic tool transitions improve feedback quality
why_fails: Reward hacking via tool misuse is possible
Large action spaces increase exploration difficulty

cards.title: Long-horizon TIR (Credit Assignment Frontier)
cards.summary: Main bottleneck is assigning credit to specific tool calls in long, interdependent trajectories.
cards.what_it_is: For LLM agents, the frontier is not basic tool calling but correctly assigning credit across long multi-turn tool chains.
cards.how_it_works: Instead of only final success/failure reward, compute more granular per-turn credit so early tool choices can be rewarded or penalized appropriately. Train policy with step-level advantages while balancing exploration vs reward hacking.

example.code:
traj = rollout_long_horizon(policy, env)
step_rewards = score_each_turn(traj)          # dense/process reward
advantages = compute_turn_advantages(step_rewards, traj.values)
update_policy(policy, traj.states, traj.actions, advantages)

papers.title: Temporal credit assignment background (Pignatelli et al., 2024)
papers.label: Temporal credit assignment…

why_works: More precise guidance for long chains
Less reliance on fragile end-only reward
why_fails: Incorrect shaping can punish useful exploration

cards.title: Memory Baseline: Static RAG-style Memory
cards.summary: Memory is an external store with handcrafted retrieval/storage heuristics.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, memory is an external context store (vector DB/notes/graph) queried at inference time, with fixed retrieval/write rules.
cards.how_it_works: Pipeline is rule-based: store by predefined policy, retrieve by similarity or graph heuristics, then stuff retrieved context into the prompt. The LLM does not learn memory-control actions like write/update/delete.
why_works: Simple and stable engineering pattern
Good first-order recall boost

cards.title: RL-guided Retrieval and Memory Management
cards.summary: RL starts controlling retrieval and basic memory operations.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, memory becomes an action space: the agent learns when to retrieve and, in stronger systems, how to write/update/delete memory.
cards.how_it_works: At each turn, a memory policy chooses operations such as RETRIEVE, ADD, UPDATE, DELETE, or NOOP. Downstream task reward (quality + efficiency) supervises these decisions, so memory behavior adapts to task demands.

example.code:
for t in range(T):
    mem_action = memory_policy.sample(state)   # RETRIEVE/ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP
    memory_state = apply_memory_action(memory_state, mem_action)
    answer, reward = answer_agent.step(state, memory_state)
    update_memory_policy(mem_action, reward)

why_works: Adapts memory behavior to task demands
Improves long-context utility over fixed heuristics
why_fails: Memory action credit assignment is hard
Can over-store or over-prune without regularization

cards.title: Structured Memory under RL (Frontier)
cards.summary: Move from token buffers to graph/atomic/hierarchical memory with learned control.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, the next step is learned control over structured memory objects (graphs, entities, timelines), not just flat retrieval snippets.
cards.how_it_works: Treat structural edits as policy actions: create nodes, link facts, abstract clusters, prune stale edges. Use long-horizon task reward to train which structural updates improve future retrieval and reasoning.

example.code:
for t in range(T):
    # 1) Memory-controller acts first (state curation step)
    mem_op = struct_mem_policy.sample(state, memory_graph)
    #    e.g., ADD_NODE, LINK, ABSTRACT_CLUSTER, DELETE_EDGE
    memory_graph = apply_graph_op(memory_graph, mem_op)

    # 2) Task-agent then reasons over updated memory
    action = agent_policy.sample(state, memory_graph)
    next_state, task_reward = env.step(action)

    # 3) Credit memory operation by downstream impact
    mem_reward = task_reward - mem_op_cost(mem_op)
    update_struct_mem_policy(mem_op, mem_reward)
    update_agent_policy(action, task_reward)
    state = next_state

why_works: Encodes temporal and relational dependencies better
Potentially stronger retrieval precision and reasoning support
why_fails: Much harder dynamics than flat memory

cards.title: Verbal Self-correction
cards.summary: Prompt-only generate->critique->revise loops improve outputs in-session without weight updates.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, self-correction starts as an inference-time loop: generate, critique in language, then revise without changing model weights.
cards.how_it_works: Use prompt scaffolds (and optionally tools) to run one or more critique/revision passes inside a single session. Gains are immediate but ephemeral because no parameter update happens.

example.code:
draft = llm(task_prompt)
critique = llm(f'Find likely errors in:
{draft}')
revised = llm(f'Revise using critique:
{critique}
Original:
{draft}')
return revised

why_works: Cheap quality gains at inference
Easy to bolt onto existing models
why_fails: Can become verbose without true correction

cards.title: Internalized Self-correction with RL/DPO
cards.summary: Train model parameters on reflective feedback so correction ability persists.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, self-correction is internalized when critique/revision outcomes are turned into training signals that update model weights.
cards.how_it_works: Collect better-vs-worse revisions (or scalar rewards), then train with DPO/RL objectives so the base model learns to produce improved outputs directly on future tasks.

example.code:
def collect_revision_pairs(tasks):
    pairs = []
    for task in tasks:
        draft = actor(task)
        critique = critic_or_prompt(f'Find errors:\n{draft}')
        revised = actor(f'Revise using critique:\n{critique}\nDraft:\n{draft}')

        # rank candidates via verifier / reward model / execution result
        score_draft = score_solution(task, draft)
        score_revised = score_solution(task, revised)
        better, worse = (revised, draft) if score_revised >= score_draft else (draft, revised)
        pairs.append((task, worse, better))
    return pairs

pairs = collect_revision_pairs(train_tasks)
loss = dpo_loss(model, pairs)
optimizer.step(loss)

why_works: Builds durable self-correction behavior
Learns from own mistakes without dense labels
why_fails: Noisy feedback can teach wrong corrections

cards.title: Iterative Self-training Loops
cards.summary: Agent generates tasks/trajectories, verifies outcomes, and trains itself in closed loops.
cards.what_it_is: For LLM agents, iterative self-training means the system creates parts of its own data, feedback, and curriculum instead of relying only on fixed human datasets.
cards.how_it_works: Run a closed loop: generate tasks/trajectories, verify outcomes via execution or critics, train policy/value models, then use improved models to generate stronger next-round experience.

example.code:
for round_idx in range(R):
    tasks = propose_tasks(agent)
    trajectories = [solve_and_verify(agent, t) for t in tasks]
    agent = train_on_verified_rollouts(agent, trajectories)
    curriculum = update_curriculum(tasks, trajectories)

why_works: Scales data generation beyond static corpora
Creates continuously improving feedback loops
why_fails: Needs robust verifiers and quality control

cards.title: Meta-evolution of Reflection Ability
cards.summary: Learn not only to correct errors, but to choose and improve reflection strategies themselves.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, the frontier is learning how to reflect, not just reflecting: choose the right correction strategy per context and update that strategy over time.
cards.how_it_works: A meta-policy selects reflection mode (quick verbal check, tool execution, deeper search, or stop). Reward combines solution quality and compute cost, so reflection budget becomes a learned control policy.

example.code:
mode = meta_reflector.sample(state)  # verbal/tool/search/stop
result = run_reflection_mode(mode, state)
reward = quality(result) - beta * compute_cost(mode)
update_meta_reflector(mode, reward)

why_works: Adapts reflection budget to task difficulty
Improves both learning efficiency and solution quality

cards.title: Fast Reasoning (System-1 style)
cards.summary: One-pass, heuristic next-token inference prioritizes speed and fluency.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, fast reasoning is direct single-pass generation: minimal explicit deliberation, maximum speed.
cards.how_it_works: Model decodes answer tokens immediately from prompt context. Any reliability improvement usually comes from lightweight heuristics (confidence checks, simple reranking), not heavy multi-step reasoning loops.

example.code:
answer = llm(prompt, max_tokens=256, temperature=0.2)
return answer

why_works: Low latency and high throughput
Good for routine pattern-matching tasks

cards.title: Slow Reasoning (Structured Deliberation)
cards.summary: Explicit intermediate reasoning, verification, and longer trajectories improve robustness.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, slow reasoning is explicit multi-step deliberation with intermediate checks before committing to an answer.
cards.how_it_works: Model generates and tests intermediate reasoning steps (plan, verify, revise), optionally with tools/search. Training and inference both allocate extra compute to reduce logical/factual error on hard tasks.

example.code:
trace = llm('Plan solution steps for: ' + problem)
for _ in range(K):
    trace = llm('Verify and revise this trace:\n' + trace)
answer = llm('Produce final answer from trace:\n' + trace)
return answer

why_works: Higher accuracy on complex knowledge-intensive tasks
Supports explicit verification and correction
why_fails: Risk of overthinking and unnecessary chain expansion

cards.title: Reasoning Frontier: Adaptive Fast/Slow Hybrid
cards.summary: Learn a controller that decides how much thinking is needed per query.
cards.what_it_is: For LLMs, hybrid reasoning learns when to answer fast vs when to trigger deeper deliberation, so accuracy and latency are optimized jointly.
cards.how_it_works: A controller estimates uncertainty/difficulty and routes each query to fast or slow path. Policy is trained against a quality-cost objective so expensive deliberation is reserved for cases where it pays off.
why_works: Combines efficiency with reliability
Avoids always-fast and always-slow extremes
why_fails: Mis-specified costs can bias toward wrong mode
